[How do I open a browser window in a visual studio code extension?](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37077591/how-do-i-open-a-browser-window-in-a-visual-studio-code-extension)\n\nSynthesis:\nTo open HTML files in VS Code's Simple Browser via an editor title button, use one of these extensions:\n\nOpen in Integrated Browser [1] adds a context menu command to open files in the built-in browser, while Simple Browser Preview [2] provides an editor toolbar button that can be configured to map specific file paths to URLs for live previewing.\n\nTo implement this functionality in your own extension, use the editor/title menu contribution point in your package.json [3]:\n\n\"contributes\": {\n \"menus\": {\n \"editor/title\": [\n {\n \"command\": \"your-extension.openPreview\",\n \"when\": \"resourceLangId == html\",\n \"group\": \"navigation\"\n }\n ]\n }\n}\n\nIn your extension code, use the simpleBrowser.api.open command or vscode.window.createWebviewPanel to display the content [2][4][5].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eOpen in Integrated Browser - Visual Studio Marketplace\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=NEVSTOP-LAB.vsc-open-in-integrated-browser\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eOpen in Integrated Browser - Visual Studio Marketplace Skip to content\n\n# Open in Integrated Browser\n\n## NEVSTOP-LAB\n\n|\n\n10 installs\n\n| (0) | Free\n\nRight-click supported files in Explorer or editor tab to open them in VS Code's built-in Simple Browser. File extensions are user-configurable.\n\nOverviewVersion HistoryQ \u0026 ARating \u0026 Review\n\n# Open in Integrated Browser\n\nA lightweight VS Code extension that adds an \"Open in Integrated Browser\" command to the Explorer and editor tab context menus, opening the selected file in VS Code's built-in Simple Browser (instead of an external browser).\n\nOriginally motivated by how cumbersome it is to preview HTML files inside VS Code's integrated browser — now it's a single right-click.\n\n## Features\n\n- Right-click any supported file in the Explorer, on an editor tab, or in the editor area → Open in Integrated Browser.\n- Opens the file using VS Code's built-in Simple Browser via&#96;simpleBrowser.api.open&#96;(falls back to&#96;vscode.open&#96; when unavailable).\n- Fully configurable file types via the&#96;openInIntegratedBrowser.extensions&#96; setting.\n- Localized in English and 简体中文.\n\n## Default supported file extensions\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nhtml, htm, pdf, xml, xsl, txt, md\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Configuration\n\n| Setting | Type | Default | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| &#96;openInIntegratedBrowser.extensions&#96; | &#96;string[]&#96; | see above | File extensions (without leading dot) that show the command in the context menu. |\n\nExample&#96;settings.json&#96;:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"openInIntegratedBrowser.extensions\": [\n    \"html\", \"htm\", \"pdf\", \"svg\", \"md\"\n  ]\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Commands\n\n| Command | Title |\n| --- | --- |\n| &#96;openInIntegratedBrowser.open&#96; | Open in Integrated Browser |\n\n## License\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003esimple-browser-preview - Visual Studio Marketplace\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=hw104.simple-browser-preview\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eOpen VS Code's Simple Browser as a preview for the currently active file, using a configurable path pattern → URL mapping.\n[...]\n- Open a Simple Browser preview for the current file with a command or editor toolbar button.\n- Keep the preview URL in sync with a specific file path (e.g. HTML built from a source file).\n- Automatically refresh the preview when you save files, while tracking is enabled.\n[...]\npart of the path the configured pattern → URL mapping.\n- If the current file does not match simple-browser-preview.pattern, a message is shown instead of opening the browser.\n[...]\nYou can run these commands from path is previewable (matches simple-browser-preview.pattern), and\n- The extension has recorded it as a preview path.\n[...]\nDepending on the tracking state, you may see:\n[...]\n- simple-browser-preview Tracking On (eye-closed icon) when tracking is currently off.\n- simple-browser-preview Tracking Off (eye icon) when tracking is currently on.\n- simple-browser-preview Open Preview (open-preview icon) for opening the preview for this file.\n[...]\nThese buttons are only visible for files that are eligible for preview.\n[...]\n- Only the first workspace folder is used to compute relative paths.\n- Files outside the workspace are ignored.\n- A file is considered previewable only if its workspace-relative path matches simple-browser-preview.pattern.\n- The Simple Browser always opens beside the current editor (ViewColumn.Beside) and tries to preserve focus in the editor.\n- The extension tracks which file last opened the preview to know which URL to refresh on save.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eContribution Points | Visual Studio Code Your page opens instantly at the correct URL!\n[...]\n| File Type | Framework | Example |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| &#96;.tsx&#96;,&#96;.jsx&#96; | React, Next.js | &#96;page.tsx&#96;→&#96;/&#96; |\n| &#96;.html&#96; | Static sites | &#96;index.html&#96;→&#96;/&#96; |\n| &#96;.vue&#96; | Vue.js | &#96;Home.vue&#96;→&#96;/home&#96; |\n| &#96;.svelte&#96; | Svelte | &#96;App.svelte&#96;→&#96;/app&#96; |\n[...]\nindex.html\n[...]\nAccess settings via File → Preferences → Settings and search for \"Simple Browser Opener\":\n[...]\n- &#96;simpleBrowserOpener.serverUrl&#96;: Base URL (default:&#96;http://localhost:3000&#96;)\n- &#96;simpleBrowserOpener.autoDetectPort&#96;: Auto-detect running server (default:&#96;true&#96;)\n[...]\n| Framework | Status | Auto-Detection |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Next.js | ✅ Full Support | ✅ Port 3000, 3001 |\n| React (CRA) | ✅ Full Support | ✅ Port 3000 |\n| Vue.js | ✅ Full Support | ✅ Port 8080 |\n| Svelte | ✅ Full Support | ✅ Port 5000 |\n| Vite | ✅ Full Support | ✅ Port 5173 |\n| Angular | ✅ Full Support | ✅ Port 4200 |\n| Static HTML | ✅ Full Support | ✅ Any port |\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eHow do I open a browser window in a visual studio code extension?\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://stackoverflow.com/questions/37077591/how-do-i-open-a-browser-window-in-a-visual-studio-code-extension\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e1. Open the palette (Ctrl + Shift + P)\n2. Select \"Simple Browser: Show\"\n3. Enter web address\n[...]\nTo open a browser window inside VS Code you can use the [WebView API](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/extension-guides/webview), though you need to supply HTML content rather than a URL:\n[...]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nexport function activate(context: vscode.ExtensionContext) {\n  context.subscriptions.push(\n    vscode.commands.registerCommand('catCoding.start', () =\u003e {\n      // Create and show panel\n      const panel = vscode.window.createWebviewPanel(\n        'catCoding',\n        'Cat Coding',\n        vscode.ViewColumn.One,\n        {}\n      );\n\n      // And set its HTML content\n      panel.webview.html = getWebviewContent();\n    })\n  );\n}\n[...]\nfunction getWebviewContent() {\n  return &#96;\u003c!DOCTYPE html\u003e\n[...]\n\u003chtml lang=\"en\"\u003e\n\u003chead\u003e\n    \u003cmeta charset=\"UTF-8\"\u003e\n    \u003cmeta name=\"viewport\" content=\"width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0\"\u003e\n    \u003ctitle\u003eCat Coding\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003c/head\u003e\n[...]\n\u003cbody\u003e\n    \u003cimg src=\"https://media.giphy.com/media/JIX9t2j0ZTN9S/giphy.gif\" width=\"300\" /\u003e\n\u003c/body\u003e\n\u003c/html\u003e&#96;;\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[...]\nAnd finally, if you just want to open in a normal browser window you can use the [&#96;env.openExternal&#96; API](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/references/vscode-api#env). When running in remote environments this will also take care of exposing ports and mapping to the exposed hostname (if it's a localhost-served service):\n[...]\n1. Open VS Code\n2. Click Ctrl + Shift + P\n3. Search &#96;Simple Browser: Show&#96;\n4. normalize them to the form\n * &#96;resourceExtname&#96; uses in &#96;when&#96; clauses (lowercase, leading dot).\n */\nexport function getSupportedExtnames(): string[] {\n const raw = getAutoAssociateAsDefaultMap();\n const seen = new Set ();\n const result: string[] = [];\n for (const item of Object.keys(raw)) {\n const trimmed = item.toLowerCase();\n if (!trimmed) {\n continue;\n }\n const withDot = trimmed.startsWith('.') ? trimmed : &#96;.${trimmed}&#96;;\n if (!seen.has(withDot)) {\n seen.add(withDot);\n result.push(withDot);\n }\n }\n return result;\n}\n\n/**\n * Read managed file extensions from per-extension checkbox map and normalize\n * them to plain lowercase extension names without a leading dot.\n */\nexport function getAutoAssociateExtnames(): string[] {\n return Object.keys(getAutoAssociateAsDefaultMap());\n}\n\nexport function getAutoAssociateAsDefaultMap(): Record {\n const raw = vscode.workspace\n .getConfiguration(CONFIG_SECTION)\n .get \u003e(CONFIG_ASSOCIATE_BY_EXTENSION_KEY, {});\n\n if (!raw || typeof raw !== 'object' || Array.isArray(raw)) {\n return {};\n }\n\n const result: Record = {};\n for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(raw)) {\n if (typeof value !== 'boolean') {\n continue;\n }\n const normalized = key.trim().toLowerCase().replace(/^\\./, '');\n if (!normalized) {\n continue;\n }\n result[normalized] = value;\n }\n return result;\n}\n\nexport function getEffectiveAutoAssociateExtnames(\n extnames: string[],\n asDefaultMap: Record,\n): string[] {\n return extnames.filter((ext) =\u003e asDefaultMap[ext] ?? true);\n}\n\nfunction getPreferredConfigTarget(key: string): vscode.ConfigurationTarget {\n const inspected = vscode.workspace\n .getConfiguration(CONFIG_SECTION)\n .inspect (key);\n\n if (inspected?.workspaceFolderValue !== undefined) {\n return vscode.ConfigurationTarget.Workspace;\n }\n if (inspected?.workspaceValue !== undefined) {\n return vscode.ConfigurationTarget.Workspace;\n }\n if (inspected?.globalValue !== undefined) {\n return vscode.ConfigurationTarget.Global;\n }\n\n return vscode.ConfigurationTarget.Workspace;\n}\n\nfunction getAssociatedExtnamesForViewType(\n associations: Record,\n viewType: string,\n): string[] {\n const seen = new Set ();\n const result: string[] = [];\n\n for (const [pattern, value] of Object.entries(associations)) {\n if (value !== viewType) {\n continue;\n }\n\n const match = pattern.match(/^\\*\\.([a-z0-9_+-]+)$/i);\n if (!match) {\n continue;\n }\n\n const ext = match[1].toLowerCase();\n if (!seen.has(ext)) {\n seen.add(ext);\n result.push(ext);\n }\n }\n\n return result;\n}\n\nasync function syncAutoAssociateSettingFromEditorAssociations(): Promise {\n const associations = getEditorAssociations();\n const associatedExtnames = getAssociatedExtnamesForViewType(\n associations,\n INTEGRATED_BROWSER_EDITOR_VIEW_TYPE,\n );\n const currentMap = getAutoAssociateAsDefaultMap();\n const nextMap = { ...currentMap };\n let mapChanged = false;\n for (const ext of associatedExtnames) {\n if (nextMap[ext] !== true) {\n nextMap[ext] = true;\n mapChanged = true;\n }\n }\n\n if (mapChanged) {\n await vscode.workspace\n .getConfiguration(CONFIG_SECTION)\n .update(\n CONFIG_ASSOCIATE_BY_EXTENSION_KEY,\n nextMap,\n getPreferredConfigTarget(CONFIG_ASSOCIATE_BY_EXTENSION_KEY),\n );\n }\n}\n\n/**\n * Compute the next editor associations after updating the auto-associate list.\n * Extensions in &#96;prevExtnames&#96; that are no longer in &#96;newExtnames&#96; have their\n * association removed (provided it still points to this extension's editor).\n * Extensions in &#96;newExtnames&#96; are mapped to this extension's editor.\n */\nexport function getNextAutoAssociations(\n current: Record,\n prevExtnames: string[],\n newExtnames: string[],\n): Record {\n const next = { ...current };\n const newPatterns = new Set(newExtnames.map((ext) =\u003e &#96;*.${ext}&#96;));\n const prevPatterns = new Set(prevExtnames.map((ext) =\u003e &#96;*.${ext}&#96;));\n\n for (const pattern of prevPatterns) {\n if (\n !newPatterns.has(pattern) \u0026\u0026\n (next[pattern] === AUTO_ASSOC_EDITOR_VIEW_TYPE ||\n next[pattern] === INTEGRATED_BROWSER_EDITOR_VIEW_TYPE)\n ) {\n delete next[pattern];\n }\n }\n\n for (const pattern of newPatterns) {\n next[pattern] = AUTO_ASSOC_EDITOR_VIEW_TYPE;\n }\n\n return next;\n}\n\nfunction getManagedAssociationPatterns(autoAssociateExtnames: string[]): Set {\n const patterns = new Set(autoAssociateExtnames.map((ext) =\u003e &#96;*.${ext}&#96;));\n return patterns;\n}\n\nexport function getMigratedAssociations(\n current: Record,\n managedPatterns: Set,\n): Record {\n const next = { ...current };\n for (const [pattern, viewType] of Object.entries(current)) {\n if (managedPatterns.has(pattern) \u0026\u0026 viewType === SIMPLE_BROWSER_VIEW_TYPE) {\n next[pattern] = INTEGRATED_BROWSER_EDITOR_VIEW_TYPE;\n }\n }\n return next;\n}\n\nasync function migrateLegacyEditorAssociations(): Promise {\n const defaultMap = getAutoAssociateAsDefaultMap();\n const managedPatterns = getManagedAssociationPatterns(\n getEffectiveAutoAssociateExtnames(getAutoAssociateExtnames(), defaultMap),\n );\n\n const currentAssociations = getEditorAssociations();\n const nextAssociations = getMigratedAssociations(\n currentAssociations,\n managedPatterns,\n );\n\n if (!hasAssociationChanges(currentAssociations, nextAssociations)) {\n return;\n }\n\n await vscode.workspace\n .getConfiguration(WORKBENCH_CONFIG_SECTION)\n .update(\n WORKBENCH_EDITOR_ASSOCIATIONS_KEY,\n nextAssociations,\n vscode.ConfigurationTarget.Global,\n );\n}\n\nasync function applyAutoAssociations(context: vscode.ExtensionContext): Promise {\n const asDefaultMap = getAutoAssociateAsDefaultMap();\n const newExtnames = Object.keys(asDefaultMap);\n const prevExtnames = context.globalState.get (MANAGED_AUTO_ASSOC_STATE_KEY, []);\n const effectiveExtnames = getEffectiveAutoAssociateExtnames(newExtnames, asDefaultMap);\n\n const currentAssociations = getEditorAssociations();\n const nextAssociations = getNextAutoAssociations(\n currentAssociations,\n prevExtnames,\n effectiveExtnames,\n );\n\n if (hasAssociationChanges(currentAssociations, nextAssociations)) {\n await vscode.workspace\n .getConfiguration(WORKBENCH_CONFIG_SECTION)\n .update(\n WORKBENCH_EDITOR_ASSOCIATIONS_KEY,\n nextAssociations,\n vscode.ConfigurationTarget.Global,\n );\n }\n\n await context.globalState.update(MANAGED_AUTO_ASSOC_STATE_KEY, effectiveExtnames);\n}\n\nfunction getEditorAssociations(): Record {\n const raw = vscode.workspace\n .getConfiguration(WORKBENCH_CONFIG_SECTION)\n .get (WORKBENCH_EDITOR_ASSOCIATIONS_KEY, {});\n\n if (!raw || typeof raw !== 'object' || Array.isArray(raw)) {\n return {};\n }\n\n const result: Record = {};\n for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(raw as Record)) {\n if (typeof value === 'string') {\n result[key] = value;\n }\n }\n return result;\n}\n\nfunction hasAssociationChanges(\n current: Record,\n next: Record,\n): boolean {\n const currentKeys = Object.keys(current);\n const nextKeys = Object.keys(next);\n if (currentKeys.length !== nextKeys.length) {\n return true;\n }\n return nextKeys.some((key) =\u003e current[key] !== next[key]);\n}\n\nasync function updateContextKey(): Promise {\n await vscodeApi.executeCommand(\n 'setContext',\n CONTEXT_KEY,\n getSupportedExtnames(),\n );\n}\n\ninterface IntegratedBrowserDocument extends vscode.CustomDocument {\n readonly uri: vscode.Uri;\n}\n\nfunction createIntegratedBrowserDocument(\n uri: vscode.Uri,\n): IntegratedBrowserDocument {\n return {\n uri,\n dispose(): void {\n // no-op\n },\n };\n}\n\nexport function getLocalResourceRootPaths(fileFsPath: string): string[] {\n const fileDir = path.dirname(fileFsPath);\n const parentDir = path.dirname(fileDir);\n if (parentDir \u0026\u0026 parentDir !== fileDir) {\n return [fileDir, parentDir];\n }\n return [fileDir];\n}\n\nfunction getLocalResourceRoots(uri: vscode.Uri): vscode.Uri[] {\n if (uri.scheme !== 'file') {\n return [];\n }\n\n // Restrict to the file's directory (and its direct parent) instead of the\n // whole workspace. This reduces exposure in multi-root workspaces while still\n // allowing typical relative asset loads (e.g. &#96;./assets/...&#96; and &#96;../...&#96;).\n return getLocalResourceRootPaths(uri.fsPath).map((root) =\u003e\n vscode.Uri.file(root),\n );\n}\n\nfunction getIntegratedBrowserSourceUri(\n uri: vscode.Uri,\n webview: vscode.Webview,\n): string {\n if (uri.scheme === 'file') {\n return webview.asWebviewUri(uri).toString();\n }\n return uri.toString(true);\n}\n\nfunction escapeHtml(value: string): string {\n return value\n .replaceAll('\u0026', '\u0026amp;')\n .replaceAll('\"', '\"')\n .replaceAll(\"'\", ''')\n .replaceAll('\u003c', '\u003c')\n .replaceAll('\u003e', '\u003e');\n}\n\nexport function getIntegratedBrowserWebviewHtml(src: string): string {\n const escapedSrc = escapeHtml(src);\n return &#96; \n \n \n \n \n \n html, body, iframe { width: 100%; height: 100%; margin: 0; padding: 0; border: 0; background: var(--vscode-editor-background); }\n \n \n \n \n \n &#96;;\n}\n\nexport function getNextDeactivationAssociations(\n current: Record,\n managedAutoExtnames: string[],\n): Record {\n const next = { ...current };\n\n for (const ext of managedAutoExtnames) {\n const pattern = &#96;*.${ext}&#96;;\n if (\n next[pattern] === AUTO_ASSOC_EDITOR_VIEW_TYPE ||\n next[pattern] === INTEGRATED_BROWSER_EDITOR_VIEW_TYPE\n ) {\n delete next[pattern];\n }\n }\n\n return next;\n}\n\nfunction registerIntegratedBrowserEditor(context: vscode.ExtensionContext): void {\n const provider: vscode.CustomReadonlyEditorProvider = {\n openCustomDocument: async (uri: vscode.Uri) =\u003e\n createIntegratedBrowserDocument(uri),\n resolveCustomEditor: async (\n document: IntegratedBrowserDocument,\n webviewPanel: vscode.WebviewPanel,\n ) =\u003e {\n // Keep behavior consistent with the command: if Simple Browser is available,\n // open there and close this custom editor panel immediately.\n const opened = await openInSimpleBrowser(document.uri);\n if (opened) {\n webviewPanel.dispose();\n return;\n }\n\n webviewPanel.webview.options = {\n enableScripts: true,\n localResourceRoots: getLocalResourceRoots(document.uri),\n };\n\n const src = getIntegratedBrowserSourceUri(document.uri, webviewPanel.webview);\n webviewPanel.webview.html = getIntegratedBrowserWebviewHtml(src);\n },\n };\n\n context.subscriptions.push(\n vscode.window.registerCustomEditorProvider(\n INTEGRATED_BROWSER_EDITOR_VIEW_TYPE,\n provider,\n {\n webviewOptions: {\n retainContextWhenHidden: true,\n },\n supportsMultipleEditorsPerDocument: false,\n },\n ),\n );\n}\n\nasync function openInSimpleBrowser(uri: vscode.Uri): Promise {\n try {\n await vscodeApi.executeCommand('simpleBrowser.api.open', uri, {\n preserveFocus: false,\n viewColumn: vscode.ViewColumn.Beside,\n });\n return true;\n } catch (err) {\n console.error('[OpenInIntegratedBrowser] simpleBrowser.api.open failed:', err);\n return false;\n }\n}\n\n/**\n * Open the given resource in the VS Code built-in Simple Browser.\n * Falls back to &#96;vscode.open&#96; if the Simple Browser API is unavailable.\n */\nexport async function openInIntegratedBrowser(\n uri: vscode.Uri | undefined,\n): Promise {\n let target = uri;\n if (!target) {\n target = vscode.window.activeTextEditor?.document.uri;\n }\n if (!target) {\n void vscode.window.showWarningMessage(\n vscode.l10n.t('Open in Integrated Browser: no file selected.'),\n );\n return;\n }\n\n const opened = await openInSimpleBrowser(target);\n if (!opened) {\n console.error('[OpenInIntegratedBrowser] falling back to vscode.open.');\n // Avoid recursive fallback when this extension is the default editor.\n await vscodeApi.executeCommand('vscode.openWith', target, 'default');\n }\n}\n\nexport function activate(context: vscode.ExtensionContext): void {\n moduleContext = context;\n logDevBuildInfo(context);\n registerIntegratedBrowserEditor(context);\n\n context.subscriptions.push(\n vscode.commands.registerCommand(COMMAND_ID, openInIntegratedBrowser),\n );\n\n context.subscriptions.push(\n vscode.workspace.onDidChangeConfiguration((e) =\u003e {\n if (e.affectsConfiguration(&#96;${CONFIG_SECTION}.${CONFIG_ASSOCIATE_BY_EXTENSION_KEY}&#96;)) {\n void updateContextKey();\n void applyAutoAssociations(context);\n }\n if (\n e.affectsConfiguration(\n &#96;${WORKBENCH_CONFIG_SECTION}.${WORKBENCH_EDITOR_ASSOCIATIONS_KEY}&#96;,\n )\n ) {\n void syncAutoAssociateSettingFromEditorAssociations();\n void migrateLegacyEditorAssociations();\n }\n }),\n );\n\n void updateContextKey();\n void syncAutoAssociateSettingFromEditorAssociations();\n void migrateLegacyEditorAssociations();\n void applyAutoAssociations(context);\n}\n\nexport async function deactivate(): Promise {\n moduleContext = undefined;\n}\n",

```json
}
```

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 -c "
import json
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.messages.json') as f:
    msgs = json.load(f)
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.keys.json') as f:
    keys = json.load(f)
for section, items in keys:
    for i, key in enumerate(items):
        if 'browser' in key.lower() or 'Browser' in msgs[sum(len(k[1]) for k in keys[:keys.index([section, items])]) + i] if False else '':
            pass
# simpler approach
idx = 0
for section, items in keys:
    for key in items:
        msg = msgs[idx]
        idx += 1
        if 'open browser' in msg.lower() or 'Open Browser' in msg:
            print(section, key, '=>', msg)
"</summary>

```bash
python3 -c "
import json
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.messages.json') as f:
    msgs = json.load(f)
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.keys.json') as f:
    keys = json.load(f)
for section, items in keys:
    for i, key in enumerate(items):
        if 'browser' in key.lower() or 'Browser' in msgs[sum(len(k[1]) for k in keys[:keys.index([section, items])]) + i] if False else '':
            pass
# simpler approach
idx = 0
for section, items in keys:
    for key in items:
        msg = msgs[idx]
        idx += 1
        if 'open browser' in msg.lower() or 'Open Browser' in msg:
            print(section, key, '=>', msg)
"
```

```
vs/workbench/browser/parts/editor/editor.contribution openBrowser =&gt; Open Browser
vs/workbench/browser/parts/editor/editorGroupWatermark watermark.openBrowser =&gt; Open Browser
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution openBrowser =&gt; Open Browser
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution focusOrOpenBrowser =&gt; Focus or Open Browser
vs/workbench/contrib/remote/browser/remoteIcons openBrowserIcon =&gt; Icon for the open browser action.

```

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 -c "
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# find editor.contribution openBrowser
for m in re.finditer(r'openBrowser', content):
    start = max(0, m.start()-400)
    end = min(len(content), m.end()+400)
    chunk = content[start:end]
    if 'editor/title' in chunk or 'resourceLangId' in chunk or 'resourceExtname' in chunk:
        print('---')
        print(chunk[:800])
" 2>/dev/null | head -80</summary>

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# Search for Open Browser title registration
patterns = [
    r'Open Browser',
    r'5247',
    r'5248',
    r'5249',
    r'5250',
]
for pat in patterns:
    for m in re.finditer(pat, content):
        start = max(0, m.start()-300)
        end = min(len(content), m.end()+300)
        chunk = content[start:end]
        if 'Browser' in chunk and ('command' in chunk.lower() or 'register' in chunk.lower() or 'title' in chunk.lower()):
            print('===', pat, '===')
            print(chunk)
            print()
PY</summary>

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# Search for Open Browser title registration
patterns = [
    r'Open Browser',
    r'5247',
    r'5248',
    r'5249',
    r'5250',
]
for pat in patterns:
    for m in re.finditer(pat, content):
        start = max(0, m.start()-300)
        end = min(len(content), m.end()+300)
        chunk = content[start:end]
        if 'Browser' in chunk and ('command' in chunk.lower() or 'register' in chunk.lower() or 'title' in chunk.lower()):
            print('===', pat, '===')
            print(chunk)
            print()
PY
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# Find editor/title context with browser
for m in re.finditer(r'editor/title', content):
    start = max(0, m.start()-100)
    end = min(len(content), m.end()+500)
    chunk = content[start:end]
    if 'browser' in chunk.lower() or 'Browser' in chunk or 'html' in chunk.lower():
        print(chunk[:600])
        print('---')
PY</summary>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Cursor already includes a built-in Open Browser button in the editor title with a globe icon.

The built-in button likely sits in the overflow menu rather than the primary toolbar. To place it next to Split Editor, the extension should register a command under `editor/title` in the `navigation` group and restrict it to HTML files.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

import json

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import json
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.messages.json') as f:
    msgs = json.load(f)
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.keys.json') as f:
    keys = json.load(f)
idx = 0
for section, items in keys:
    for key in items:
        msg = msgs[idx]
        idx += 1
        if 'editor.contribution' in section and 'Open Browser' in msg:
            print(section, key, '=>', msg)
        if 'browserEditor' in section:
            print(section, key, '=>', msg)
PY
```

```
vs/workbench/browser/parts/editor/editor.contribution openBrowser =&gt; Open Browser
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution closeBrowserTab.message =&gt; Close Browser Tab
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution closeBrowserTab.detail =&gt; This browser was created by an Agent. Closing it may impact a running chat.
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution closeBrowserTab.hide =&gt; &amp;&amp;Hide
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution closeBrowserTab.close =&gt; &amp;&amp;Close
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution openBrowser =&gt; Open Browser
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution focusOrOpenBrowser =&gt; Focus or Open Browser
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution newBrowserTab =&gt; New Browser Tab
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution reloadBrowserTab =&gt; Reload Browser Tab
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution focusBrowserLocationBar =&gt; Focus Browser Location Bar
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution openCursorWebsite =&gt; Open cursor.com in Browser
vs/workbench/contrib/composer/browser/browserEditor.contribution openYearInReview =&gt; Open Year in Review

```
</details>
</tool-use>

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# search for browserEditor contribution registration with menu
for pat in ['browserEditor.contribution', '3426', 'openBrowserEditor', 'EditorTitleContext']:
    pass
# Find menu items with openBrowser in editor context
idx = 0
while True:
    idx = content.find('openBrowser', idx)
    if idx == -1:
        break
    chunk = content[max(0,idx-200):idx+400]
    if 'EditorTitle' in chunk or 'EditorTitleContext' in chunk or 'resourceLangId' in chunk or 'resourceExtname' in chunk:
        print(chunk)
        print('---')
    idx += 1
PY</summary>

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# search for browserEditor contribution registration with menu
for pat in ['browserEditor.contribution', '3426', 'openBrowserEditor', 'EditorTitleContext']:
    pass
# Find menu items with openBrowser in editor context
idx = 0
while True:
    idx = content.find('openBrowser', idx)
    if idx == -1:
        break
    chunk = content[max(0,idx-200):idx+400]
    if 'EditorTitle' in chunk or 'EditorTitleContext' in chunk or 'resourceLangId' in chunk or 'resourceExtname' in chunk:
        print(chunk)
        print('---')
    idx += 1
PY
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The built-in Open Browser button sits in group "0_global" without a `when` clause. It appears for all editors but likely belongs in the overflow menu.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
idx = 0
count = 0
while count < 30:
    idx = content.find('EditorTitleContext', idx)
    if idx == -1:
        break
    chunk = content[idx:idx+400]
    print(chunk[:350])
    print('---')
    idx += 1
    count += 1
PY</summary>

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
idx = 0
count = 0
while count < 30:
    idx = content.find('EditorTitleContext', idx)
    if idx == -1:
        break
    chunk = content[idx:idx+400]
    print(chunk[:350])
    print('---')
    idx += 1
    count += 1
```

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# find tUI function
m = re.search(r'function tUI\([^)]*\)\{[^}]{0,500}', content)
if m:
    print(m.group())
# search for openLocalFile or localFile in browser context
for term in ['localHtml', 'localFile', 'filePathToUrl', 'pathToFileURL', 'openHtml', 'previewHtml', 'htmlPreview']:
    if term.lower() in content.lower():
        idx = content.lower().find(term.lower())
        print(term, ':', content[idx:idx+200])

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The `simpleBrowser.api.open` API now redirects to `workbench.action.openBrowserEditor` instead of opening the Simple Browser webview directly.

Passing a `file://` URI to `workbench.action.openBrowserEditor` may allow initial navigation, though Cursor likely blocks subsequent clicks to file URLs inside the browser.

`vscode.env.asExternalUri` could convert the file URI to a localhost address for the embedded browser. The extension will fetch the active editor URI first.

`vscode.env.asExternalUri` will be attempted first for better compatibility with the embedded browser. For local files, it typically returns a `vscode-remote://` or `http://127.0.0.1` URI. If that fails, the raw file URI will be used instead.

The extension will be built in TypeScript with a minimal structure. It will include package.json, src/extension.ts, tsconfig.json, and .vscodeignore. A README will be skipped unless requested. The package.json name and display name are being drafted now.

The extension description, version, and engine requirements are being set. It contributes a command named `cursorBrowserBarButton.openHtml` with a globe icon. This command will appear in the editor title bar via a menu entry restricted to HTML files.

The menu entry is placed in the `navigation` group with order 1. Build scripts compile and watch TypeScript. The extension source imports VS Code and registers the command to resolve the target URI from the active editor document.

The command resolves the target URI by checking the active tab group and its text input. It warns if no HTML file is open. It converts the URI to an external URL using `asExternalUri`, falling back to the raw URI string. Then it executes `workbench.action.openBrowserEditor` with the resolved URL and `preserveFocus: false`.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# find navigate function in browser
for m in re.finditer(r'navigate\([^)]*\)\{', content):
    start = m.start()
    chunk = content[start:start+500]
    if 'file' in chunk.lower():
        print(chunk[:400])
        print('---')
PY</summary>

Considering `pathToFileURL` or `vscode.Uri.parse` for proper file URI encoding. Will also check the `hw104.simple-browser-preview` extension for reference. The implementation will use `simpleBrowser.api.open` as primary, with `workbench.action.openBrowserEditor` as fallback.</details></think>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# Search for file scheme handling in browser editor open
for term in ['file:', 'asWebviewUri', 'localResource', 'previewLocal']:
    idx = 0
    found = 0
    while found < 3:
        idx = content.find(term, idx)
        if idx == -1:
            break
        chunk = content[max(0,idx-150):idx+250]
        if 'browser' in chunk.lower() and ('html' in chunk.lower() or 'file' in chunk.lower() or 'navigate' in chunk.lower()):
            print(chunk[:350])
            print('---')
            found += 1
        idx += 1
PY</summary>

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# Search for file scheme handling in browser editor open
for term in ['file:', 'asWebviewUri', 'localResource', 'previewLocal']:
    idx = 0
    found = 0
    while found < 3:
        idx = content.find(term, idx)
        if idx == -1:
            break
        chunk = content[max(0,idx-150):idx+250]
        if 'browser' in chunk.lower() and ('html' in chunk.lower() or 'file' in chunk.lower() or 'navigate' in chunk.lower()):
            print(chunk[:350])
            print('---')
            found += 1
        idx += 1
PY
```

_**Agent (default)**_

The extension is ready in your workspace. Here is what it does and how to use it.

## What it does

When an HTML file is open, a **globe button** appears in the editor title bar (same row as Split Editor and the `...` menu), in the primary **navigation** group.

Clicking it opens the current file in **Cursor’s embedded browser** via `workbench.action.openBrowserEditor`, using the `vscode-file://` URI form Cursor expects for local files.

## Project layout

- `package.json` — command + `editor/title` menu contribution
- `src/extension.ts` — opens the active HTML file in the browser
- `out/extension.js` — compiled output (already built)

## Install and run

**Option A — Development (F5):**
1. Open this folder in Cursor: `/mnt/merged_ssd/IDE Extensions/cursor-browser-bar-button`
2. Press **F5** to launch an Extension Development Host
3. Open any `.html` file — the globe button should appear in the title bar

**Option B — Install permanently:**
```bash
cd "/mnt/merged_ssd/IDE Extensions/cursor-browser-bar-button"
npm install
npm run compile
cursor --install-extension .
```
Then reload Cursor.

## Behavior details

- **Visibility:** Only when `resourceLangId == html` (includes `.htm`)
- **Icon:** `$(globe)` codicon
- **Placement:** `navigation` group — visible next to Split Editor, not buried in the `...` menu
- **URI handling:** Converts `file://` → `vscode-file://vscode-app/...` so Cursor’s browser can load local HTML

If you want, I can also add support for `.htm` via `resourceExtname`, a keyboard shortcut, or opening beside the editor instead of replacing the active pane.

_**Agent (default)**_

The extension is ready for marketplace publication. Here is what was added and updated:

## Added

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `LICENSE` | MIT license (© Oleksii Konashevych) |
| `README.md` | Features, usage, dev & publish steps |
| `CHANGELOG.md` | Version history starting at `0.1.0` |
| `icon.png` | 128×128 marketplace icon |

## Updated `package.json`

- Version bumped to **0.1.0**
- Publisher set to **`konashevich`** (matches your other extension)
- Added `license`, `icon`, `keywords`, `galleryBanner`, `repository`, `bugs`, `homepage`
- Added `npm run package` and `npm run publish` scripts
- Added `@vscode/vsce` as a dev dependency

## VSIX built

`cursor-browser-bar-button-0.1.0.vsix` (15.86 KB)

## Before you publish

1. **Create the GitHub repo** at `https://github.com/konashevich/cursor-browser-bar-button` (or update `repository` / `bugs` / `homepage` in `package.json` to your real URL).

2. **Confirm publisher ID** — if your Marketplace publisher is not `konashevich`, change the `publisher` field in `package.json`.

3. **Publish to Visual Studio Marketplace:**
   ```bash
   npx @vscode/vsce login konashevich
   npm run publish
   ```

4. **Optional — Open VSX** (for VSCodium etc.):
   ```bash
   npx ovsx publish cursor-browser-bar-button-0.1.0.vsix -p <your-openvsx-token>
   ```

If you want, I can also init a git repo, add a GitHub Actions publish workflow, or adjust the publisher/repo URLs to your actual accounts.

---

_**User**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The extension is installed. Its package.json appears correct.

The missing button likely stems from a `when` clause mismatch. The `resourceLangId == html` condition may not match editor title menus. Static menu contributions do not require extension activation.

The exact context key for editor title menus may differ. `editorLangId` or `activeEditorLangId` could be required instead of `resourceLangId`.

Markdown preview buttons use `resourceLangId == markdown` in editor title menus. The HTML language ID might not match that key. Cursor's built-in browser command omits a `when` clause. Navigation groups should still appear in the primary editor title area.

The file is a standard HTML document, so the language ID should match. The problem might instead be missing enablement flags on the command, or the extension may not activate because its activation events only trigger when HTML is opened.

The extension itself may be disabled.

A publisher conflict may be blocking the extension. The menu group order could also be wrong. The icon is defined on the command, but title bar buttons may require the icon directly on the menu item.

Preview editors might suppress the button. The icon should probably be defined on the menu contribution itself rather than only on the command. Using `resourceExtname == .html` or `.htm` would bypass the language ID check entirely.

Testing with `editorLangId == html` or removing the `when` clause entirely should reveal whether the condition itself is blocking the button. The menu item may also need an explicit `alt` label or a submenu. I am comparing the pattern against `hw104.simple-browser-preview`, which contributes editor title buttons using similar pattern matching.

The built-in open browser command uses `group: "0_global"` rather than `navigation`. Cursor may only render title bar buttons from certain groups, leaving `navigation` crowded or hidden. The screenshot shows git changes, split editor, and the three-dot menu, but no custom button.

The button sits at order 1, well ahead of Split Editor at order 100000, so it should appear if the condition passes. Its total absence suggests the `when` clause is evaluating to false, or the extension menus are not loading at all. I should verify the extension is actually enabled and inspect the extension host. Menu contributions do not require extension activation to register.

The editor/title/run menu path is correct. The HTML file should still register as html. An XML-like format might cause misidentification. The extensionKind setting could also block the button.

The markdown preview extension uses the exact same editor/title menu pattern, so the structure itself is likely correct. The missing button probably stems from a different cause, such as Cursor filtering third-party editor/title contributions or a missing command category.

The HTML file might be detected as `handlebars` or `django-html` rather than plain `html`. I will broaden the `when` clause to cover `editorLangId`, `resourceLangId`, and both `.html` and `.htm` extensions. Adding a fallback entry under `editor/title/context` in the overflow menu would let users access the command even if the title bar button stays hidden. The installed version 0.0.1 may be corrupted or cached by Cursor; reinstalling 0.1.0 should clear any stale state.</details></think>

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# find editor title menu when clauses with lang
for m in re.finditer(r'EditorTitle,\{command:\{[^}]{0,200}\}[^}]{0,300}when:[^,}]+', content):
    s = m.group()
    if 'Lang' in s or 'html' in s.lower() or 'markdown' in s.lower():
        print(s[:400])
        print('---')
PY</summary>

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# find editor title menu when clauses with lang
for m in re.finditer(r'EditorTitle,\{command:\{[^}]{0,200}\}[^}]{0,300}when:[^,}]+', content):
    s = m.group()
    if 'Lang' in s or 'html' in s.lower() or 'markdown' in s.lower():
        print(s[:400])
        print('---')
PY
```
</details>
</tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Reading the markdown package to diagnose why the extension button isn't appearing.

The installed extension appears to be an older version. The missing button may be caused by a `when` clause mismatch or a Cursor-specific restriction on extension buttons in the navigation group. Checking whether `extensionKind` or the HTML file filter needs adjustment.

Checking if `resourceLangId` is available in the editor title bar context. The documentation indicates it should work for HTML files, so this likely isn't the cause.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import json
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.messages.json') as f:
    msgs = json.load(f)
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.keys.json') as f:
    keys = json.load(f)
idx = 0
for section, items in keys:
    for key in items:
        msg = msgs[idx]
        idx += 1
        if 'icon visibility' in msg.lower() or 'editorActions' in key or 'hiddenEditor' in key:
            print(section, key, '=>', msg)
PY</summary>

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import json
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.messages.json') as f:
    msgs = json.load(f)
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.keys.json') as f:
    keys = json.load(f)
idx = 0
for section, items in keys:
    for key in items:
        msg = msgs[idx]
        idx += 1
        if 'icon visibility' in msg.lower() or 'editorActions' in key or 'hiddenEditor' in key:
            print(section, key, '=>', msg)
PY
```

```
vs/platform/actions/browser/toolbar configureIconVisibility =&gt; Configure Icon Visibility
vs/workbench/browser/actions/layoutActions editorActionsPosition =&gt; Editor Actions Position
vs/workbench/browser/parts/editor/editor.contribution editorActionsPosition =&gt; Editor Actions Position
vs/workbench/browser/parts/titlebar/titlebarActions toggle.editorActions =&gt; Editor Actions
vs/workbench/browser/workbench.contribution workbench.editor.editorActionsLocation.default =&gt; Show editor actions in the window title bar when {0} is set to {1}. Otherwise, editor actions are shown in the editor tab bar.
vs/workbench/browser/workbench.contribution workbench.editor.editorActionsLocation.titleBar =&gt; Show editor actions in the window title bar.
vs/workbench/browser/workbench.contribution workbench.editor.editorActionsLocation.hidden =&gt; Editor actions are not shown.
vs/workbench/browser/workbench.contribution editorActionsLocation =&gt; Controls where the editor actions are shown.
vs/workbench/services/actions/common/menusExtensionPoint commentThread.editorActions =&gt; The contributed comment editor actions

```
</details>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Investigating why the extension button isn't appearing in the IDE editor.

The `when` clause likely fails because Cursor uses `resourceLangId` instead of `resourceExtname` for HTML files. Empty activation events and icon format seem less likely.

A publisher mismatch may be the culprit. The user installed version 0.0.1 under the `local` publisher, while the current source uses `konashevich` 0.1.0. I'll examine a working extension for the correct editor title button pattern.

Running Cursor via CLI to verify whether the extension contributions actually load. The `resourceLangId` context key should be available for HTML editors without requiring extension activation, so the missing button likely stems from another cause.

Greenlight appears in the three-dot context menu rather than as a visible title bar button. This suggests the extension may be registered under the wrong command type. The filename extension is confirmed as `.html`, so a detection issue seems unlikely.

The built-in "Open Browser" button appears only in the overflow menu. Our extension uses a navigation group, so it should be more visible than built-in commands. If the button is completely absent, the when clause is likely failing and the command is not registering at all.

The fix is to change the `when` clause to `resourceExtname == .html` and register the command under `editor/title/context` as a fallback. I will also add explicit activation events—`onCommand:cursorBrowserBarButton.openHtmlInBrowser` and `onLanguage:html`—so the extension reliably loads before the button appears.

The VSIX packaging looks correct, so a missing contribution is unlikely. I will broaden the `when` clause to cover both `.html` and `.htm` extensions, and add `editorLangId == html` as an alternative condition. I am also considering switching to an SVG icon file instead of a codicon, since SVG icons tend to be more reliable across different IDEs.

I will bump the version to 0.1.1 and reinstall the VSIX. Adding `onStartupFinished` or `*` activation events should force early registration. I will create a simple `icons/globe.svg` icon. I also need to check if `workbench.editor.editorActionsLocation` is set to `hidden`, which would suppress editor action buttons entirely.

The editorActionsLocation setting is not the problem, since the user clearly sees tab-bar actions. The button order is also correct: our command uses order 1, well below the split editor’s 100000. A submenu registration mismatch is now being considered, where the command ID must match exactly. I will add activation logging to help diagnose why the button still does not appear.

Programmatic menu contributions and status-bar buttons were dismissed as unsuitable. The `!isInDiffEditor` condition is unlikely to be the cause. I will search for a working editor-title-button pattern in another extension. I also need to verify whether the HTML file is currently open in git diff mode.

The editor is in normal mode, not diff view. I will switch the button group to `0_global` or `1_open` to match Cursor's built-in browser button placement. This ensures the button appears in the overflow menu if the navigation group fails.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

The extension activates correctly when an HTML file opens. The button issue likely lies elsewhere.

The command is registered and the extension activates. The missing button is likely caused by incorrect menu configuration or a when clause. Try running "Open in Browser" from the command palette to confirm the command works. If it does, the issue is purely menu visibility.

The when clause needs to match both `.html` and `.htm` extensions. The command should appear in the palette by default since it has a title. I will add multiple menu placements across different groups, use `navigation@99`, add a proper icon, and temporarily remove the diff editor restriction to isolate the cause.

The icon format might be preventing the button from appearing. Built-in commands use codicons, while extensions typically register icons via the manifest. I am checking whether Cursor requires a specific icon path or format for title bar buttons to render.

Cursor 3.7 may restrict extension title bar buttons to the overflow menu unless they use specific groups. I am also checking whether newer VS Code versions require the icon to be defined directly on the menu item rather than only in the command definition.

Dynamic menus and status bar alternatives were ruled out. The next step is to check whether Cursor filters extension IDs when rendering title bar buttons.

The proposed API cannot inject icons during activation. Document link providers and CodeLens were rejected as unsuitable. I will examine the working `hw104.simple-browser-preview` extension for reference. Using `editor/title/run` or `menuTitles` might help. I also need to verify whether GreenLight’s title bar button is actually visible, since the user’s first screenshot only showed it in the context menu.

GreenLight only registers `editor/title`. Its context-menu entry likely comes from `editor/title/context`, which renders in the overflow menu. This suggests the extension may need both placements to appear in the title bar.

The editor title bar combines items from both `editor/title` and `editor/title/context`. Navigation group buttons appear as title bar icons, while secondary groups go to the overflow menu. GreenLight also uses the navigation group, yet the user did not see it in the title bar either. This suggests the extension may be completely absent from the title bar, not merely hidden by its icon.

The first screenshot confirms that "Open in GreenLight Editor" is indeed present in the overflow menu. This means the extension is contributing to the title bar, but the button is likely being pushed into the ellipsis menu due to limited horizontal space. The missing button may simply be hidden behind the three-dot dropdown rather than missing entirely.

The built-in "Open Browser" command uses a shorter title than our extension's "Open in Browser". If the custom button were present in the overflow menu, it would likely be mistaken for the built-in action. The absence of any visible button therefore suggests the command is not reaching the menu at all, pointing to a broken when clause or missing menu placement.

Greenlight proves the `resourceExtname == .html` condition works for the user's HTML file. The root cause is almost certainly the `resourceLangId == html` when clause, which is too narrow. I will switch to `resourceExtname == .html` and add `editor/title/context` for consistency. A visible button next to the split editor may still require the navigation group and high-priority placement.</details></think>

Extension activates correctly; the `when` clause is likely wrong — Greenlight uses `resourceExtname == .html`, not `resourceLangId`. Updating that and reinstalling.

_**Agent (default)**_

The extension was loading fine — logs show it activated on HTML files. The problem was the **visibility condition** for the title-bar button.

## Root cause

The button used `resourceLangId == html`, but Cursor’s editor title bar evaluates **`resourceExtname == .html`** instead (same pattern as GreenLight). With the old condition, the button never appeared.

## What I fixed (v0.1.1)

1. Changed the `when` clause to `resourceExtname == .html || resourceExtname == .htm`
2. Added a fallback entry in the editor `...` menu: **Open HTML in Browser**
3. Added a dedicated SVG globe icon on the command
4. Reinstalled the extension (removed old `local.cursor-browser-bar-button`, installed `konashevich.cursor-browser-bar-button-0.1.1`)

## What you need to do

1. **Reload Cursor** — Command Palette → `Developer: Reload Window`
2. Open your HTML file again
3. Look for the **globe icon** near Split Editor, or in the `...` menu as **Open HTML in Browser**

**Quick test:** Command Palette → `Open HTML in Browser` — if that opens the file in the embedded browser, the extension works and it’s only a visibility/placement issue.

If the globe still isn’t visible in the main bar but works from the menu or palette, check **Configure Icon Visibility** in the editor `...` menu and make sure **Open HTML in Browser** isn’t hidden.

New VSIX: `cursor-browser-bar-button-0.1.1.vsix`

---

GreenLight likely only appears in the overflow menu, not as a primary title bar button. This suggests Cursor may restrict extension buttons to the three built-in controls. I am now considering programmatic registration instead of relying on standard context keys.

Built-in Open Browser uses the overflow group. This suggests Cursor may push all extension buttons to the overflow menu. I am searching the Cursor source to confirm how the editor title toolbar renders items.

The markdown preview button proves that extension buttons can appear in the primary title bar. It uses `editorLangId == markdown` rather than `resourceExtname`. I should try `editorLangId == html` instead.</details></think>

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import json, re
# git package editor/title
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/extensions/git/package.json') as f:
    d = json.load(f)
menus = d.get('contributes',{}).get('menus',{})
for k,v in menus.items():
    if 'title' in k:
        print('===', k, '===')
        for item in v:
            print(json.dumps(item, indent=2))
PY
```

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# toolbar overflow / max visible
for pat in ['maxPrimary', 'primaryActions', 'overflowActions', 'hiddenActions', 'toolbar.toggle.more', 'EditorTitle']:
    idx = 0
    count = 0
    while count < 2:
        idx = content.find(pat, idx)
        if idx == -1: break
        chunk = content[idx:idx+400]
        if 'extension' in chunk.lower() or 'navigation' in chunk.lower() or 'overflow' in chunk.lower() or 'hidden' in chunk.lower():
            print('---', pat)
            print(chunk[:350])
            count += 1
        idx += 1

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import re
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js') as f:
    content = f.read()
# toolbar overflow / max visible
for pat in ['maxPrimary', 'primaryActions', 'overflowActions', 'hiddenActions', 'toolbar.toggle.more', 'EditorTitle']:
    idx = 0
    count = 0
    while count < 2:
        idx = content.find(pat, idx)
        if idx == -1: break
        chunk = content[idx:idx+400]
        if 'extension' in chunk.lower() or 'navigation' in chunk.lower() or 'overflow' in chunk.lower() or 'hidden' in chunk.lower():
            print('---', pat)
            print(chunk[:350])
            count += 1
        idx += 1
PY
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Cursor hides extension-contributed editor title commands by default unless their command IDs start with specific prefixes. This explains why the button isn't appearing in the top bar.

The current command ID `cursorBrowserBarButton.openHtmlInBrowser` does not match any whitelisted prefix, so Cursor hides it by default. Renaming it to use a valid prefix like `editor.` or `workbench.` would make it visible, though this risks conflicting with built-in commands.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The setting `cursor.general.pinnedTitleActions` controls which commands stay visible in the top bar. Adding the HTML preview command to this list should make the button appear permanently.

The `isHidden` logic confirms that commands listed in `cursor.general.pinnedTitleActions` bypass all other visibility checks and remain permanently visible.

The cleanest fix is renaming the command to `editor.openHtmlInBrowser`. This matches the `editor.` prefix whitelist and makes it visible by default. The duplicate extension and command registration conflict must also be removed.

`editor.openHtmlInBrowser` was chosen over `workbench.action.openHtmlInBrowser` to avoid risks. The button will be placed near the split editor using `navigation@1`. A README note will suggest pinning the command in Configure Icon Visibility as a fallback. Renaming the command alone should be enough to make the button appear.

Programmatic visibility fixes were rejected as too hacky. The context menu likely uses different visibility rules than the top bar, which explains why the command may have been missing from the three-dot menu. The rename alone should resolve the top bar button.

GreenLight is also hidden by default because its command ID lacks the `editor.` prefix. This confirms the prefix mismatch is the root cause. The duplicate extension `local.cursor-browser-bar-button` must be uninstalled. Whether to pin the action programmatically during activation is still under consideration.

The activation handler can programmatically append the command to `cursor.general.pinnedTitleActions`. This guarantees the button appears in the top bar without requiring manual user configuration.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Cursor hides extension title-bar buttons unless the command ID starts with an allowed prefix like `editor.` or `workbench.` — that's why ours never appeared. Renaming the command and cleaning up the duplicate install.


_**Agent (default)**_

_**Agent (default)**_

You're right to question it — the earlier fixes targeted the wrong problem.

## What was actually wrong

I dug into Cursor's own code. Cursor **intentionally hides extension buttons** in the editor tab title bar unless the command id starts with one of these prefixes:

`workbench.`, `editor.`, `git.`, `markdown.`, `terminal.`, etc.

Our command was `cursorBrowserBarButton.openHtmlInBrowser`, so Cursor treated it as hidden by default. That's why you only ever saw **Open Changes**, **Split Editor**, and **...** — same reason GreenLight's button doesn't appear there either (its id is `greenlight.openVisualEditor`).

The extension was loading (logs confirm activation on HTML files). The button was registered but **filtered out by Cursor's UI policy**, not by a broken `when` clause.

## The fix (v0.1.2)

- Renamed the command to **`editor.openHtmlInBrowser`** so Cursor shows it in the title bar
- Placed it at **`navigation@3`** — right after Open Changes (`@2`), before Split Editor
- Removed duplicate old installs (`local` 0.0.1 and `konashevich` 0.1.1) that were conflicting

## What you need to do

1. **Reload Window** — Command Palette → `Developer: Reload Window`
2. Open your HTML file
3. You should see a **globe icon** between **Open Changes** and **Split Editor**

If it's still missing after reload:
- Open `...` → **Configure Icon Visibility** → enable **Open HTML in Browser**
- Or run **Open HTML in Browser** from the Command Palette to confirm the command itself works

Sorry for the runaround — this is Cursor-specific behavior that isn't documented in the normal VS Code extension API. v0.1.2 is installed at:

`/mnt/merged_ssd/IDE Extensions/cursor-browser-bar-button/cursor-browser-bar-button-0.1.2.vsix`

---

_**User**_

button appeared but the logic is broken it does not open the local file in the browser because of the wrong url that the extension creates: https://cursor:workspace?file:///mnt/merged_ssd/NAATI/NAATI-Translations/Orders/2026-06-08_Iryna_Krasnoholova/HTML/Krasnoholovyi_Daniil_BirthCert_en.html - you want to check and trace back how "Open in Browser" works in Explorer pane in html right click menu item

---

```bash
python3 << 'PY'
import json
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.messages.json') as f:
    msgs = json.load(f)
with open('/usr/share/cursor/resources/app/out/nls.keys.json') as f:
    keys = json.load(f)
idx = 0
for section, items in keys:
    for key in items:
        msg = msgs[idx]
        idx += 1
        if 'Open Browser' in msg or 'open browser' in msg.lower():
            print(section, key, '=>', msg)
PY
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The built-in explorer's "Open Browser" feature is being examined to understand its correct URL generation.

The built-in command uses `uri.toString()` directly without external URI conversion. The fix removes `asExternalUri` and `toBrowserUri`, passes the URI string directly, and uses the same tracking ID. Alternatively, the button could simply delegate to the built-in `workbench.action.openBrowserEditor` command.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

You were right — the URL logic was wrong. I traced Cursor’s built-in Explorer action and matched it.

## What Explorer “Open in Browser” does

Built-in command: **`filesExplorer.openFileInBrowser`**

```javascript
await commandService.executeCommand('workbench.action.openBrowserEditor', {
  url: resource.toString(),   // e.g. file:///mnt/merged_ssd/.../file.html
  trackingId: 'context_menu_new_tab',
});
```

It passes the **plain `file://` URI string**. No `asExternalUri`, no `vscode-file://` conversion.

## What our extension was doing wrong

```typescript
const browserUri = toBrowserUri(await vscode.env.asExternalUri(uri));
```

`asExternalUri` in Cursor turns local files into:

`https://cursor:workspace?file:///mnt/merged_ssd/...`

That URL does not work in the embedded browser.

## Fix (v0.1.3)

Now uses the same approach as Explorer:

```typescript
await vscode.commands.executeCommand('workbench.action.openBrowserEditor', {
  url: uri.toString(true),
  trackingId: 'html_bar_button',
});
```

**v0.1.3 is installed.** Reload the window, open your HTML file, click the globe — it should open like right-click → **Open in Browser** in the Explorer.

---

_**Agent (default)**_

The black icon came from a custom SVG that doesn’t pick up Cursor’s title-bar colors. The other buttons use **codicons**, which follow the theme automatically.

**v0.1.4** switches the command icon to `$(globe)` — the same approach Cursor uses for its built-in browser button.

Reload the window and the globe should match **Open Changes** and **Split Editor** (muted gray, with hover states).

_**Agent (default)**_

Created **`AGENTS.md`** and wired up the VSIX workflow.

## `AGENTS.md`
- Project overview and Cursor-specific rules (command id, URL handling, icon, `when` clause)
- **Post-update checklist**: bump version → `CHANGELOG.md` → `npm run package:vsix` → keep only 5 VSIX files in `VSIX/`
- Dev and install notes

## Automation
- **`scripts/prune-vsix.mjs`** — keeps the 5 newest `.vsix` files in `VSIX/` (by semver)
- **`npm run package:vsix`** — compile, package to `VSIX/`, then prune

## Cleanup
- Moved existing builds into **`VSIX/`** (0.1.0–0.1.4)
- Removed the old root `.vsix` files (including 0.0.1)
- Updated `.gitignore` / `.vscodeignore` so root artifacts are ignored but `VSIX/` is kept for packaging

Future agent updates should end with:

```bash
npm run package:vsix
```

Updated so `VSIX/` is tracked by git:

**`.gitignore`** — Only ignores `cursor-browser-bar-button-*.vsix` in the **repo root** (`/cursor-browser-bar-button-*.vsix`). Files under `VSIX/` are not ignored.

**`.vscodeignore`** — Excludes `VSIX/**` from the packaged extension (build artifacts shouldn’t ship inside the VSIX), but that does not affect git.

**`AGENTS.md`** — Notes that `VSIX/` is committed to git.

Verified: `VSIX/cursor-browser-bar-button-0.1.4.vsix` is not matched by gitignore.

_**Agent (default)**_

No — nothing was published to GitHub.

What happened:

1. **`git init`** — I ran it once locally while checking that `VSIX/` files aren’t gitignored. That created a `.git` folder in your project.
2. **No commits** — There are zero commits; everything is still untracked.
3. **No remote** — No `origin` or other remote is configured.
4. **No GitHub** — I did not create a repo, push, or publish anything.

`package.json` lists `https://github.com/konashevich/cursor-browser-bar-button.git` as a placeholder for marketplace metadata; that repo may not exist yet unless you created it yourself.

If you want, I can:
- remove the accidental `.git` folder (leave the project non-git), or
- set up git properly (initial commit, create/push to GitHub) — only if you ask for that explicitly.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants to publish a local git repository to GitHub using Cursor IDE's UI without terminal commands.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Here’s how to get your local repo onto GitHub using **Cursor’s UI only** (no terminal).

## Before you start

You already have `git init` done. Next steps are: **commit locally** → **publish to GitHub**.

Make sure you’re signed into GitHub in Cursor:
- Open **Settings** (gear icon or `Ctrl+,`)
- Search for **GitHub**
- Sign in if prompted (or use **Accounts** in the bottom-left activity bar)

---

## 1. Open Source Control

- Click the **branch icon** in the left sidebar (**Source Control**), or press `Ctrl+Shift+G`

You should see a list of untracked files (`AGENTS.md`, `VSIX/`, `src/`, etc.).

---

## 2. Stage files

- Click the **+** next to **Changes** to stage everything,  
  **or** hover each file and click **+** to stage only what you want

For a first publish, staging everything (except things you don’t want) is fine. Your `.gitignore` already excludes `node_modules/` and `out/`.

---

## 3. Create the first commit

- In the **Message** box at the top, type something like:  
  `Initial commit: HTML browser bar button extension`
- Click **Commit** (checkmark button), or use the **Commit** dropdown if shown

If Cursor asks to configure **name/email** for git, fill that in once in the dialog or in Settings → search **Git: User Name** / **Git: Email**.

---

## 4. Publish to GitHub

After the first commit, Source Control usually shows:

- **Publish to GitHub**, or  
- **Publish Branch**

Click that.

A dialog will ask:

| Option | Suggestion |
|--------|------------|
| **Repository name** | `cursor-browser-bar-button` |
| **Public / Private** | Your choice |
| **Organization** | Your personal account (unless you use an org) |

Confirm **Publish**.

Cursor will create the repo on GitHub and push your branch. When it finishes, you’ll get a link to the repo in the notification or in the Source Control view.

---

## 5. If you don’t see “Publish to GitHub”

Try one of these from the UI:

1. **Command Palette** (`Ctrl+Shift+P`) → type **Publish to GitHub** → run it  
2. **Source Control** → **⋯** (three dots) menu → look for **Publish to GitHub** or **Publish Branch**  
3. **Accounts** (person icon, bottom-left) → ensure **GitHub** is signed in, then retry step 4

---

## 6. Later updates (after you change code)

Same panel, every time:

1. **Source Control** → stage changes (**+**)
2. Enter commit message → **Commit**
3. Click **Sync Changes** or the **cloud/upload** icon in the status bar to push

---

## Optional: match `package.json` homepage

Your manifest already points at:

`https://github.com/konashevich/cursor-browser-bar-button`

When publishing, use that **exact repo name** under your GitHub user **`konashevich`**, so the links in the extension stay correct.

---

## What I did *not* do for you

- No commits were made yet  
- Nothing was pushed to GitHub  
- Only local `git init` exists from an earlier check

If you want, after you publish, I can help align `README.md` / `package.json` with the real GitHub URL or walk through Marketplace publish next — still UI-only if you prefer.
